metadata.description: Knowledge base derived from peer-reviewed cyber hygiene literature for a knowledge graph-grounded conversational agent.

sources.title: Human Factors in Remote Work: Examining Cyber Hygiene Practices
sources.notes: Primary source for cyber hygiene categories.

sources.title: Exploratory Study on Remote Work and Cyber Hygiene
sources.venue: Journal of Information Security and Applications

cyber_hygiene_categories.name: Authentication and credential hygiene
cyber_hygiene_categories.definition: Cyber hygiene practices related to passwords and authentication.

cyber_hygiene_categories.name: Email and messaging hygiene
cyber_hygiene_categories.definition: Cyber hygiene practices related to email and messaging security.

threats.name: Phishing
threats.definition: A social engineering attack designed to trick users into revealing sensitive information or executing malicious actions.

practices.name: Enable multifactor authentication
practices.description: Users should enable MFA on important accounts whenever possible.

practices.name: Verify sender identity
practices.description: Users should verify sender addresses and domains before responding to emails.

knowledge_units.title: Strong passwords and MFA reduce credential compromise
knowledge_units.claim: Users should create strong and unique passwords and enable multifactor authentication to reduce the risk of credential compromise.
knowledge_units.explanation: Weak or reused passwords increase the likelihood that attackers can gain unauthorized access to accounts. Multifactor authentication provides an additional security layer even if credentials are stolen.
recommended_actions: Use a unique password for every account.
Use a password manager to generate and store passwords.
Enable multifactor authentication on important services.
Avoid reusing passwords across work and personal accounts.

evidence.exact_supporting_text: Changing default usernames and passwords, creating complex usernames and passwords and enabling multifactor authentication.

knowledge_units.title: Verifying email senders reduces phishing risk
knowledge_units.claim: Users should verify sender identity, inspect email domains, and avoid interacting with suspicious links or attachments.
knowledge_units.explanation: Phishing attacks often impersonate trusted organizations using deceptive email addresses and malicious links.
recommended_actions: Inspect grammar and urgency cues in emails.
Verify unexpected requests through secondary communication channels.

evidence.exact_supporting_text: Checking the subject of an incoming email, checking the email domain name of the sender and checking the spelling and grammar of email requests.

validity_facts.fact: Users should inspect suspicious email domains.
Users should avoid interacting with suspicious links.

avoid_claims: All phishing emails contain spelling mistakes
Email security tools alone are sufficient
